lead: A moving day in Hadleigh calls for a little extra thought, because this is a medieval wool town with a long historic High Street and timber-framed buildings that were never designed for furniture lorries. After years of carrying homes into Hadleigh from our base in Westerfield, we know exactly where the day can stall and how to keep it flowing. This checklist walks you through the run-up, the morning and your first hours in the new home, with the practical detail that makes a Hadleigh move calm rather than chaotic.

sections.h: Two Weeks Out: Sort the Access First
sections.body:
In most towns the first big job before a move is packing. In Hadleigh, it is access. If your new home sits on or near the historic High Street, you need to work out well ahead of time where a removal lorry can actually stop and unload. The street is long, busy and old, parking does not always suit a big vehicle, and the side streets can be tight. Tell us about it early and we will plan, perhaps shuttling from a larger vehicle parked nearby, or timing the unload to avoid the busiest part of the day.

If your new place is on one of the newer streets around the edge of Hadleigh, or out in one of the IP7 villages, life is usually simpler, with driveways and room to manoeuvre, though country lanes are worth a quick check for width and surface. Either way, this is the fortnight to confirm your moving date with us, start running down the freezer, notify the important people of your change of address and arrange mail redirection. If your chain is looking shaky, talk to us about storage now rather than leaving it to the day.

Gather your packing materials this week too, or ask us to supply them. Good boxes, plenty of tape, bubble wrap for the fragile things and marker pens for labelling all make a real difference, and running out part way through is a reliable source of stress. Having everything in the house before you start means the packing flows steadily rather than stalling every time you reach for materials that have run out.

sections.h: One Week Before: Pack Room by Room
sections.body:
With a week to go, packing should be well under way. Work room by room rather than drifting about, and begin with the spaces you use least: the spare room, the loft, the garage and the shed. The loft is the classic trap, holding far more than anyone remembers, and finding heavy boxes up a ladder on moving morning throws the whole timetable out, which matters all the more when you have a tight unloading window booked near Hadleigh's High Street. Clear those spaces this week so nothing ambushes you on the day.

Label every box clearly on the side as well as the top, marking the room it belongs to in your new Hadleigh home and a note of the contents. We carry boxes straight to the right room as we unload, so good labels keep your things from piling up in the hall. Colour coding works beautifully: a colour per room, matching stickers on the boxes and a matching sheet on each door at the new house, so even a crew seeing the property for the first time can unload at speed, which is exactly what you want when time on a busy street is short.

Set aside anything precious, important documents, jewellery and small valuables, to travel in your own car. Mark fragile boxes on more than one side, and keep a simple numbered inventory if any part of your move involves storage or a staggered handover. By the end of this week, the only things left to pack should be the kettle, the breakfast things and the beds you are sleeping in the night before.

The day before is for tying everything off. Defrost and empty the fridge and freezer, finish the last of the packing, and strip all but tonight's beds, so that when we arrive in the morning everything is sealed and ready except the kettle and the breakfast things. The single biggest cause of a slow, expensive moving day is unfinished packing when the crew turns up, and in Hadleigh that delay can eat straight into a carefully arranged access window on the High Street, so being genuinely ready matters here more than in most places.

Pack your essentials box, the most important box of all, and keep it for your own car rather than the lorry. Into it goes the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for the children. This is the box that keeps everyone going and lets you make a brew the moment you arrive without opening the whole van. Take and note your meter readings at the old property, and charge your phone overnight.

Think through the morning route too. The centre of Hadleigh gets busy, and the historic High Street and its side streets can catch out anyone in a hurry. If your move involves driving through or near the centre, a little timing thought tonight saves frustration tomorrow. Clear a path from the front door to the kerb at both ends, and if you have children or pets, arrange care for the day or set up one cleared room as their safe base.

sections.h: Moving Morning: Making the Window Count
sections.body:
On the morning itself, being ready and being present is everything, and never more so than in a town where unloading time can be tight. Open the curtains, switch on the lights so we can see, and make sure nothing is still hidden in the loft, shed or behind a door. When we arrive we like a quick walk-round so you can point out the fragile boxes, the antiques and anything needing extra care, especially if you are moving period furniture into or out of an older Hadleigh home with narrow stairs and low doorways.

Leave out a kettle, mugs and a few biscuits for the team if you can; it keeps everyone going and it is the small kindness our crews always appreciate. Keep children and pets in their safe room with a clear note on the door, because the front door will stand open for much of the morning and cats in particular will bolt given the chance. Tell us where they are so we all keep that door shut. If parking is restricted outside, let us get straight to loading so we make full use of the available window.

Before we leave the old property, do a proper walk-through together. Check every cupboard, the loft, the shed, the garden and behind every door, because things get left in the rush. Confirm the inventory, sort the handover of the old keys and make sure you have the keys for your new Hadleigh home. Then meet us at the other end, where we will unload to the right rooms and rebuild the beds so you have somewhere comfortable to sleep that first night.

sections.h: Arriving in Hadleigh: The First Hours
sections.body:
Arriving at the new home is where all that labelling pays off. As we unload, we carry each box to the room marked on its side, so even with a tight unloading window your home quickly takes shape rather than becoming one great pile in the hall. Point us to where the big furniture goes before we bring it in; placing a wardrobe correctly first time is far easier than shuffling it once it is full, and in older properties with awkward corners that planning really counts. If you have colour-coded the doors, the whole unload moves faster and with fewer questions.

Get the kitchen working enough for a brew and a simple meal, and ask us to make up the beds before we go. After a long day, the difference between a made-up bed and a mattress buried under boxes is the difference between a good first night in Hadleigh and a miserable one. Find your essentials box, put the kettle on, and resist the temptation to unpack everything tonight. The boxes will still be there in the morning, and a proper rest is the best possible start in your new town.

Over the following days, unpack at a sensible pace, room by room, and take time to enjoy where you now live. A first wander along the historic High Street, a look at the timber-framed buildings and a walk by the River Brett are how a new house starts to feel like home. Register with a local GP and dentist, sort your utility and council accounts, and let yourself settle. We will have done our part by then, and every load we carry is fully covered, so you can put your energy into enjoying life in Hadleigh.

faqs: What should I check first before moving day in Hadleigh?

Access, before anything else. The long historic High Street and its side streets are busy with limited parking for a long lorry, so work out where a lorry can stop and tell us early. We can shuttle with a smaller vehicle or time the unload to avoid the busiest hours. Newer streets and the IP7 villages are usually far simpler.
Why does being fully packed matter so much for a Hadleigh move?

Because unloading time near the High Street is often limited by the busy street and parking. Unfinished packing when the crew arrives eats into that window. Being sealed, labelled and ready, with only the kettle and beds left, lets us load straight away and keep to the plan.
What goes in the essentials box for my Hadleigh move?

Pack the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for children. Keep it in your own car so it is to hand the moment you reach your new Hadleigh home.
Can you move period furniture into an older Hadleigh home?

Yes. We regularly move heavy and period pieces into older timber-framed and Georgian properties with narrow staircases and low doorways. Point these items out on the morning walk-round so we can pad them and plan the carry, and the move goes smoothly even in the trickier historic homes off the High Street.
